chain_of_thought: Crash occurs in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, part of libUbikeyJni.so, in an ASN.1/X509 parsing stack (Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> JNI). This strongly suggests parsing of attacker-influenced certificate/ASN.1 data.

JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser is directly in the stack, meaning Java code likely passes user or server-provided certificate (e.g., smart card, token, or network cert) into native parsing. Fuzz harness mimics this by feeding arbitrary bytes via fuzz_one_input, which implies data flow from external/untrusted input in normal use as well.

Function name Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum indicates traversal of ASN.1 structure children; crashes there are typically due to out-of-bounds indexing or malformed length/child count handling. Such logic is notoriously vulnerable if length/child count is read from input and not fully validated against available buffer size or structure bounds.

We lack exact decompilation due to tool 404 errors, but the high-level call stack and naming are characteristic of cert/ASN.1 libraries (similar to known OpenSSL/LibreSSL issues), where malformed ASN.1 can cause memory safety bugs (OOB read/write, infinite recursion, stack exhaustion). The presence of a specific Compute_* child-number routine further suggests computation based on embedded length/child fields, where incorrect bounds check could lead to memory corruption.

Because the crash appears during parsing of ASN.1 without any intervening validation layers shown in the call stack (CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum), and the fuzz harness successfully triggered a crash just by supplying data, this is consistent with a real parser bug rather than a harness artifact.

However, we do not see allocator or sanitizer frames, nor exact crash reason (SIGSEGV vs. abort). It might be an internal abort/assert on invalid child count, resulting in a DoS only, but given typical native ASN.1 parsing patterns, memory safety issues are more probable than pure logical exits. We must still keep some uncertainty.

Given the realistic data flow (Java -> JNI -> CERT_Load) and the nature of cryptographic/ASN.1 parsing, it is reasonable to classify this as at least a denial-of-service vulnerability with likely memory-safety implications exploitable by crafted certificates or ASN.1 blobs. Confidence is moderate-to-high but not absolute because we lack source/assembly confirmation of OOB access or use-after-free.
reasons: Crash in ASN.1/X509 parsing routine Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, indicating failure on malformed ASN.1 structures.

JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser passes attacker-controlled ASN.1/certificate data from Java into native CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* without evidence of strict bounds checking in the stack trace.
Malformed external input alone (fuzzer data) is sufficient to trigger a native crash, demonstrating at least a denial-of-service and likely memory safety risk in the parser.
recommendations: Review and audit ASN.1/X509 parsing code paths in libUbikeyJni.so, especially Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related functions, for bounds checks on child indices and lengths.
Add robust length and structure validation before parsing ASN.1 data from untrusted sources, and handle parse errors gracefully without crashing.
Consider integrating or replacing with a well-vetted ASN.1/X509 library that has undergone security review and fuzzing, and enable runtime hardening (ASLR, stack canaries, CFI).
assumptions: Java method jGetUser receives or builds ASN.1 or certificate data from an untrusted or semi-untrusted source (card, token, network).
Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum operates directly on attacker-influenced ASN.1 structures and its crash is not caused by an out-of-band harness violation such as impossible JNI parameters.
The crash represents abnormal termination (e.g., SIGSEGV or similar) rather than a deliberate, safe rejection path.
limitations: No decompiled native code was available due to tool access issues, so details of bounds-checking and exact memory operation are inferred from symbols and stack only.
Exact signal/exception type and register/memory state at crash were not provided, preventing precise classification of the memory error.
Java-side code for UbikeyJni.jGetUser and the format/source of its inputs were not inspected, limiting clarity on real-world triggerability.

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed ASN.1/X.509 certificate or structure passed via UbikeyJni.jGetUser
prerequisites: Attacker can provide or influence certificate/ASN.1 data consumed by UbikeyJni.jGetUser (e.g., smart card payload, network certificate, or imported key).
Application loads and parses the provided data through CERT_Load in libUbikeyJni.so.
Device allows installation or interaction with the app so that the vulnerable code path is executed.
exploit_pipeline: Craft an ASN.1/X.509 blob with malformed child count or length fields that stress Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum (e.g., excessive or inconsistent child numbers).
Deliver this blob to the application by whichever channel feeds UbikeyJni.jGetUser (e.g., uploading a certificate, presenting a malicious smart card, or manipulating a network handshake).
Ensure the app calls UbikeyJni.jGetUser, leading to CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* parsing routines handling the malformed data.

On parsing, Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum mis-handles child count/length and triggers memory corruption or a crash, causing denial of service and potentially enabling further exploitation depending on memory layout.
exploit.notes: Concrete exploitability (e.g., RCE) depends on the precise nature of the bug in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum (OOB write vs. read vs. assert). Current assessment focuses on DoS with potential for memory corruption.

chain_of_thought: Stack shows crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → JNI jGetUser. This is typical of parsing DER-encoded certificates/ASN.1 structures.

Memcpy-based crash during ASN.1/DER decoding is a classic sign of out-of-bounds read/write due to trusting length fields or miscomputed sizes. Fuzzer harness entry fuzz_one_input suggests DER/X509 blob is fuzzer-controlled and equivalent to attacker-controlled data in real world (user certs, tokens, smartcard responses, etc.).

Even without direct decompilation (tool 404 errors), reasonable inference: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST likely takes a buffer+length and walks ASN.1 TLV structures, copying fields into internal structs via memcpy. If there were strict bounds checks, fuzzer would be less likely to hit a memcpy crash; allocator/sanitizer not mentioned, direct __memcpy termination indicates invalid src/dst/len (e.g., dst from small stack/heap buffer, len from attacker-controlled ASN.1 length).

The path is JNI entry → CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → __memcpy; there is no evidence of upper-bound validation of certificate/field sizes at the JNI boundary. In typical Ubikey / smartbank context, user or external hardware can provide arbitrary certs/responses. Therefore this is realistically attacker-reachable, not a harness-only NULL misuse.

Because we lack the exact snippet, we can’t pin down whether it's write-OOB vs read-OOB, but memcpy crash almost always implies memory safety. This should be treated as a high-severity memory corruption in X.509/ASN.1 parsing, likely triggerable by malformed cert data. Confidence is slightly below 0.9 because we didn’t see code, but still high (~0.8).
reasons: Crash occurs inside __memcpy invoked from ASN.1/DER decoding routines (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST), consistent with out-of-bounds copy based on malformed length fields.
Fuzzer harness fuzz_one_input drives JNI jGetUser → CERT_Load with arbitrary data, modelling attacker-controlled certificate or token input.
No evidence of strict bounds checking on DER/ASN.1 lengths before memcpy, making memory corruption likely and not just a benign abort.
The affected library is a security-sensitive JNI module (libUbikeyJni.so) used in banking context, so malformed user-controlled certs/keys are realistic.
recommendations: Review and harden Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and related ASN.1/X.509 parsing to ensure all memcpy/memmove calls are bounded by the actual input buffer size, not just ASN.1 length fields.
Add strict length and structural validation of all DER/ASN.1 elements (including nested sequences) before copying into fixed-size buffers.

Enable and run with ASan/UBSan on native libs to localise exact memcpy site and confirm whether overflow is on source or destination, then add targeted bounds checks or dynamic allocation based on validated sizes.
assumptions: DER/X.509 or ASN.1 data processed by CERT_Load ultimately comes from attacker-controllable input (user certificates, hardware tokens, network).
The memcpy crash is due to invalid size or pointer derived from decoding logic rather than an artificial harness condition (e.g., deliberately passing null pointers).
There are no hidden, strong bounds checks in the omitted code that would convert this into a mere logic abort without memory safety impact.
limitations: Could not decompile functions due to tool access error; assessment is based on naming, stack trace, and common implementation patterns.
Exact failing memcpy call site (src/dst/len) not inspected, so we cannot state definitively whether this is OOB read, write, or both.
Java-side argument construction and validation for jGetUser could not be inspected, so taint from external inputs is inferred from typical usage of such JNI functions.

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed ASN.1/DER certificate or token blob fed into UbikeyJni.jGetUser via app logic
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process an arbitrary or crafted certificate/DER blob (e.g., importing a user cert, interacting with a compromised smartcard/U2F token, or feeding data over a network channel parsed by jGetUser).
The vulnerable libUbikeyJni.so is present and reachable via the UbikeyJni.jGetUser JNI call in the production app.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER/ASN.1-encoded certificate or token response whose length fields cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute an oversized copy for an internal buffer.
Deliver this crafted blob through the feature path that ultimately invokes UbikeyJni.jGetUser (e.g., certificate import, authentication handshake, or token interaction).
When CERT_Load calls Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT and then Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, the unchecked memcpy is executed with attacker-influenced length and/or pointers, corrupting memory.
Use repeated attempts and heap grooming (if possible) to steer the overflow towards overwrite of control data (vtable, function pointer, return address) to escalate from crash to code execution.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.kbankwith.smartbank/.MainActivity --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der  # assuming the app has a path that imports/loads the cert
adb logcat | grep -i UbikeyJni  # observe crash when the crafted cert is processed
exploit.notes: Exact data shape for the crashing ASN.1 structure is unknown; a fuzzer-guided approach (e.g., libFuzzer/AFL targeting CERT_Load) is recommended to minimise and stabilise a crashing input for PoC and further exploit development.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser -> fuzz_one_input. This is a memcpy-based crash in an ASN.1/DER decoder inside libUbikeyJni.so, which is clearly application code, not allocator or harness code.

Tools failed to locate symbol names directly (HTTP 404 responses), so I cannot decompile the exact functions; however, the function names and call stack are semantically rich: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST strongly implies custom DER ASN.1 decoding, CERT_Load implies certificate loading logic, and the JNI bridge jGetUser is an app-facing function.

Given a fuzz harness feeding arbitrary data into fuzz_one_input that eventually reaches jGetUser, it is realistic that attacker-controlled or untrusted data (e.g., certificate blobs, authentication tokens) reaches CERT_Load and then Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. The direct crash in __memcpy, with no standard library sanity abort indicated, is highly suggestive of an out-of-bounds read/write caused by incorrect length calculation or insufficient bounds checking during DER parsing.

ASN.1/DER decoding is notoriously error-prone and easy to fuzz; typical bugs include trust in attacker-controlled length fields, nested length overflows, and using memcpy with those attacker-controlled lengths without validating them against the actual buffer size. Decode_DER_ASN1_ST almost certainly processes such length fields and calls memcpy accordingly.

Because we cannot inspect the exact code, we must infer from (1) function naming, (2) direct memcpy crash, (3) custom decoder vs. system library, and (4) realistic data flow from JNI -> CERT_Load -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST that this is very likely an unsafe memcpy with lengths derived from untrusted DER data, i.e., an out-of-bounds memory access (CWE-787 / CWE-119).

The fuzz harness directly drives the JNI entrypoint but that does not mean the bug is harness-only; in production, similar untrusted blobs could be provided via network or NFC/USB tokens or remote servers. The path JNI->CERT_Load->Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is an app logic path; there is no indication that real inputs would be constrained enough to avoid the bug. There is also no sign that the crash is caused by unrealistic harness behavior such as passing null pointers into JNI that the app would never do.

Impact: an unchecked memcpy inside certificate parsing in a security-sensitive authentication library can plausibly lead to heap or stack corruption and therefore potential code execution. Thus severity is at least high. Given lack of exact instruction / buffer location, I classify as high rather than critical. Confidence is high (~0.85) but not 0.9 because we cannot see the decompiled source or confirm exact attacker control over the length parameter.

Exploitability conceptually: attacker crafts a malicious X.509/DER blob delivered through the app’s normal user/certificate input channel (e.g., QR, file import, or server response) so that Decode_DER_ASN1_ST computes an excessive length and memcpy overruns a heap buffer. The same payload that crashes under fuzzing would be adjusted to the real protocol framing in the released app.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from custom Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside app library, indicating out-of-bounds memory access during DER parsing
Call stack shows realistic app path: JNI jGetUser -> CERT_Load -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, not a harness-only or allocator-only path
ASN.1/DER decoding with memcpy is a common source of memory corruption when lengths from untrusted data are not fully validated
Fuzz harness demonstrates that arbitrary input reaching this path can trigger the crash; similar untrusted blobs are realistic in production certificate-loading scenarios
recommendations: Manually audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load for all memcpy/memmove calls and ensure lengths are strictly checked against buffer sizes and DER bounds
Add defensive bounds checks for ASN.1 length fields (including nested/constructed types) before any copy or allocation, and handle malformed data gracefully
Integrate targeted fuzzing of DER/ASN.1 inputs to CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in CI with ASan/UBSan to confirm the crash is fixed
assumptions: JNI method jGetUser accepts or indirectly processes untrusted certificate or identity blobs from user or remote server
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST uses memcpy with lengths derived from parsed DER fields without complete bounds validation
The same code path is reachable in production under realistic inputs, not gated behind debug-only flags
limitations: Could not decompile or inspect Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or CERT_Load due to tool lookup errors; reasoning is based on names and crash context
Exact buffer location (heap vs stack) and whether overwrite is write- or read-out-of-bounds are unknown
Java-side argument construction and validation around jGetUser are not visible, so precise attacker control of input length is inferred, not proven

exploit.trigger_method: Malicious DER-encoded certificate or identity blob processed through UbikeyJni.jGetUser
prerequisites: Attacker can supply or influence the certificate/identity data that jGetUser processes (e.g., via network, file import, or token)
Target device runs vulnerable version of com.kbankwith.smartbank with libUbikeyJni.so
No upstream validation fully rejects malformed or oversized DER before it reaches CERT_Load
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded ASN.1 structure whose length fields cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute a memcpy size larger than the backing buffer
Deliver the crafted DER blob through the app’s usual path that leads to UbikeyJni.jGetUser (for example, as part of a certificate or token file)
Trigger the code path where CERT_Load calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, causing the unchecked memcpy to perform an out-of-bounds write or read
Iteratively tune the payload (using local fuzzing on a test device) to move from simple crash to controlled heap corruption and potential code execution
exploit.notes: Activity and extra key names are placeholders; testers should identify the real component and argument path that causes UbikeyJni.jGetUser to invoke CERT_Load with attacker-supplied data.
